coredata.dc:description: This study explores the impact of surface chemistry and heteroatom doping on the photocatalytic activity of carbon dot (CD), with a focus on their roles in energy and electron transfer mechanisms for reactive oxygen species generation. A series of N- and S-doped CDs were synthesized and thoroughly purified. Photocatalytic singlet oxygen production was detected via endoperoxide formation, while hydroxyl radicals were monitored through peroxide-assisted hydroxylation of terephthalic acid. The findings reveal that specific CDs primarily govern either energy or electron transfer mechanisms. Fluorescence lifetime analysis suggested that long-lived photon emission generally correlates with increased singlet oxygen production, though the effects vary depending on the type of nitrogen and sulfur doping. This also resulted in an alteration from energy transfer to electron transfer processes. Doping also influences the shift from energy to electron transfer pathways. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis suggested that the partial crystallinity and the presence and arrangement of surface oxygen-containing groups, such as SO, –OH, and –COOH, may drive this transition between the two mechanisms.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The high-resolution XPS analysis and deconvolution fits of the S 2p spectra across the three (N,S)-containing samples are shown in Fig. 3b\u2013d. The S 2p spectra were fitted with two doublet structures (doublet separation between 2 p3/2 and 2p1/2 is 1.18 eV). The first 2 p3/2 peak, centered at 163.4 eV, corresponds to C-SH organic bonds, while the second 2 p3/2 peak, centered at 165.9 eV, corresponds to C-(S[dbnd]O)\u2013C organic groups [70,71]. Interestingly, the S[dbnd]O group is the dominant species only in the N,S-Meth sample (Fig. 8d), which is the sole material capable of generating ROS via electron transfer. The prominent presence of surface S[dbnd]O species may explain the switching from energy transfer to electron transfer mechanisms in CDs. This reasoning is further supported by the higher concentration of \u2013OH and \u2013COOH species in N,S-Meth compared to other (N,S)-containing samples (see Table 3), as these species also involve oxygen atoms bonded to non-carbon elements. Therefore, the preference for electron transfer in N,S-Meth is likely due to the rearrangement of bonded oxygen atoms with other heteroatoms, such as sulfur, on the surface, combined with the partial crystallinity (higher degree of ordering) observed in this sample, which is absent in other CDs. However, future work focusing on selective surface modifications, such as the adsorption of S[dbnd]O and related species on undoped and N-doped CDs, is necessary to conclusively validate this hypothesis.This work was supported by the National Science Centre (NCN), Poland, within the OPUS 21 project (2021/41/B/ST5/04533). The authors would like to thank Katarzyna Jelska-Houda from Jagiellonian University for assistance with CHNS analysis and Mateusz Marzec from AGH University of Science and Technology (Poland) for support with XPS measurement.

This work was supported by the National Science Centre (NCN), Poland, within the OPUS 20 LAP project (2020/39/I/ST5/03556). The authors would like to thank Katarzyna Jelska-Houda from Jagiellonian University for assistance with CHNS analysis and Mateusz Marzec from AGH University of Science and Technology for support with XPS measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis pathways for each type of synthesized CDs [46].

CDs purification scheme. The purification process was carried out in the following steps: (1) The product obtained after synthesis was treated with 1 M NaOH to achieve an alkaline pH; (2) the solution underwent ultrasonication for 1 h; (3) the pH was adjusted to neutral (pH = 7) using 1 M HCl; (4) the solution was filtered through a Büchner funnel; (5) the filtrate was placed in a regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane; (6) the membrane was immersed in distilled water, with water changes every 24 h; (7) the purified solution was removed from the membrane, frozen, and (8) lyophilized to obtain the final dry product [47].

Scanning electron micrographs (yellow label), transmission electron micrographs (purple label), and DLS size distribution showing the sub-6 nm primary particle size of selected CDs samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
STEM images and EDS elemental mapping of N–U (upper panel) and N,S-Meth (lower panel) carbon dots. HAADF STEM imaging was applied for the N,S-Meth sample.
Fluorescence spectra of all CDs excited by various excitation wavelengths. Measurement condition: [CDs] = 50 mg dm−3; emission slit = 4 nm.

Photocatalytic activity of synthesized CDs on the ROS generation. (a) ABDA-to-endoperoxide transformation via singlet oxygen and (b) peroxide-assisted hydroxylation of TA via hydroxyl radical in the absence and presence of EDTA.
Fluorescence decay curves of all CDs in water (a; inset shows the logarithmic scale) and corresponding calculated photon lifetimes with their normalized frequency distribution (b).
Surface-to-bulk distribution of N and S atoms in selected samples (a) and high-resolution S 2p XPS spectra for N,S-Cys (b), N,S-Glu (c), and N,S-Meth (d).
Sample designations and their corresponding synthesis conditions.
Elemental composition of CDs determined by CHNS analysis.
Elemental surface composition (atomic %) determined by XPS.

head.ce:title: Understanding photocatalytic processes in carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms: Energy or electron transfer?

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the impact of surface chemistry and heteroatom doping on the photocatalytic activity of carbon dot (CD), with a focus on their roles in energy and electron transfer mechanisms for reactive oxygen species generation. A series of N- and S-doped CDs were synthesized and thoroughly purified. Photocatalytic singlet oxygen production was detected via endoperoxide formation, while hydroxyl radicals were monitored through peroxide-assisted hydroxylation of terephthalic acid. The findings reveal that specific CDs primarily govern either energy or electron transfer mechanisms. Fluorescence lifetime analysis suggested that long-lived photon emission generally correlates with increased singlet oxygen production, though the effects vary depending on the type of nitrogen and sulfur doping. This also resulted in an alteration from energy transfer to electron transfer processes. Doping also influences the shift from energy to electron transfer pathways. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis suggested that the partial crystallinity and the presence and arrangement of surface oxygen-containing groups, such as SO, –OH, and –COOH, may drive this transition between the two mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles typically with at least one dimension smaller than 20 nm [1,2], have gained increasing attention due to their unique properties, such as tunable photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, high dispersibility in aqueous media, and simple, cost-effective synthesis. As non-metallic materials, CDs offer a highly attractive alternative to traditional metal-based semiconductor photocatalysts for a wide range of applications, including photosensitization [3,4], photopolymerization [5–7], and sensing [8,9]. Notably, their metal-free nature significantly reduces the risk of toxicity, making them ideal for use in biomedical imaging, therapeutics, and other biological applications [10–14]. In these areas, CDs can be used in generating reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (HO•), which are crucial for important processes such as disease treatment, diagnostics, and photocatalysis. This positions CDs as a key player in advancing safer and more effective photochemical applications.

Recent advances in CDs synthesis have demonstrated that incorporating heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) into CDs can significantly modulate their electronic structure, surface chemistry, and fluorescence, often leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance [15–21]. However, despite substantial progress in synthesizing N- and S-doped CDs from diverse precursors, a systematic understanding of how these heteroatoms, and their spatial distribution within the CD structure, impact the photochemical activity of CDs remains elusive. Investigating the structure-property-activity relationships of heteroatom-doped CDs is therefore essential. Simultaneously, careful attention must be paid to the removal of side products formed during synthesis, such as low molecular weight phosphors [22–25], which could misleadingly influence the observed properties and photoactivity.

Moreover, the fundamental factors governing the photocatalytic behavior of CDs remain poorly understood. The interplay between energy and electron transfer processes in CD-mediated photocatalysis is particularly intriguing. While energy transfer is often associated with tunable fluorescence [22,26–29], several studies have demonstrated that heteroatom-doped CDs can also facilitate electron transfer processes that can be used in novel applications beyond traditional CDs [30], such as photocatalytic hydrogen production [31–34] and CO2 reduction [35–37]. To address this, two critical questions should be answered: In specific CDs and CD-mediated photocatalytic reactions, does energy transfer or electron transfer predominate? And what key factors or properties regulate these mechanisms? These persistent inquiries are fundamental to unlocking the full potential of CDs in various fields. Given that singlet oxygen is typically produced via energy transfer [38], while hydroxyl radicals are predominantly generated through electron transfer pathways [39], exploring these particular ROS generation across various types of CDs should yield valuable insights into this relatively underexplored area.

This study aims to address these knowledge gaps by investigating a series of CDs with and without N and S heteroatoms. A comprehensive analysis of key properties, including photoluminescence lifetime and heteroatom distribution, has been conducted to understand the properties influencing the photocatalytic behavior of these CDs. The primary objective is to understand and elucidate the intricate relationships between CD composition, structure, and photocatalytic performance, with a particular focus on identifying the factors or properties that govern energy and electron transfer mechanisms during ROS generation.

Proper purification of CDs was crucial at this stage to obtain pristine products. The solid products from the one-pot synthesis were treated with 1 M NaOH solution (15 mL per product) and subjected to ultrasonication for 1 h to break down potential aggregates. The solutions were neutralized with 1 M HCl and filtered through a Büchner funnel to remove any remaining aggregates. Next, the solution containing CDs, fluorophores, and contaminants was centrifuged and purified using regenerated cellulose membranes (Spectra/Por® 6, MWCO 3.5 kDa) to remove low molecular weight impurities and fluorophores. The efficiency of dialysis was confirmed by spectroscopic analysis, thin-layer chromatography (TLC), and liquid chromatography with a diode-array detector (LC-DAD), as shown in Figs. S1–S3. The filtrates were concentrated by evaporation, frozen, and lyophilized to produce a dry product. The purified aqueous CD suspensions were then frozen and lyophilized to yield a dry product. The complete purification process is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

For the preparation of CA-f CDs, the solution after preliminary purification was dialyzed using regenerated cellulose membranes (Spectra/Por® 6, MWCO 10 kDa) to remove low molecular weight impurities, oligomers, and CDs with a molecular weight below 10 kDa. The resulting CD solution was then fractionated using MWCO 15 kDa regenerated cellulose membranes (Spectra/Por® 6). The filtrates were concentrated by evaporation, frozen, and lyophilized to produce a dry product.

Absorption spectra were recorded using two instruments. A StellarNet SilverNova TEC-X2 spectrophotometer was employed to acquire spectra in the range of 190–1100 nm, utilizing a UV/Vis deuterium/halogen light source. Additionally, absorption spectra were measured over the range of 200–1100 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrophotometer, with a 1.0 cm path length quartz cuvette.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on multiple instruments. Steady-state fluorescence measurements were conducted using a Horiba FluoroMax + spectrofluorometer, scanning between 360 and 700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Additional fluorescence spectra were acquired using a PerkinElmer LS-55 spectrofluorometer in a 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette, with measurements taken at various excitation wavelengths and different acetonitrile (ACN) ratios. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured with a Horiba DeltaFlex, employing a pulsed NanoLED excitation source at 370 nm. A highly diluted colloidal silica Ludox solution was used as the instrument response function (IRF) for spectral deconvolution. The fluorescence decay curves were fitted using a bi-exponential model, with a chi-squared value of less than 1.2 to determine the lifetime (τ) based on EzTime software.
Elemental analyses for carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and sulfur (CHNS) were conducted using an Elementar Vario Micro Cube analyzer.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained within the range of 4000 to 550 cm−1, with a scanning speed of 4 cm−1 s−1, over 50 cycles using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrophotometer equipped with an iD5 attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory.
The average hydrodynamic diameter dispersed in water was determined at 25 °C by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Malvern Zetasizer Ultra ZS.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a Thermo Scientific Helios 5 Hydra DualBeam, with materials deposited on silicon wafers and imaging conducted in immersion mode. High-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental analysis were performed using an FEI Tecnai Osiris, without prior sonication of the samples.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Rigaku MiniFlex 600 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The measurements were conducted with a scan step of 0.05° (2θ) and a scan speed of 3° min−1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a PHI VersaProbeII Scanning XPS system, using monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) X-rays focused on a 100 μm spot and scanned over a 400 μm × 400 μm area. The photoelectron take-off angle was 45°, with a pass energy of 117.50 eV (0.5 eV step) for survey scans and 46.95 eV (0.1 eV step) for high-resolution spectra. All XPS spectra were referenced to the C 1s peak of unfunctionalized, saturated carbon (C–C) at 285.0 eV. Spectral deconvolution was performed using PHI MultiPak software, with background subtraction applied using the Shirley method. The information depth of the XPS analysis was approximately 5 nm. The surface-to-bulk distribution percentages of N and S were calculated based on atomic carbon percentages as the internal calibration. Normalized N or S to C ratios obtained from XPS were compared to those from CHNS analysis and expressed as percentages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic singlet oxygen generation via endoperoxide formation
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of energy transfer was assessed by monitoring the photocatalytic generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) through its reaction with the water-soluble probe, 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA). The reaction between ABDA and singlet oxygen leads to anthracene endoperoxide formation, which can be tracked using UV–Vis spectroscopy. CDs samples were dispersed in a 1:1 (v/v) mixture of acetonitrile (ACN) and water, with 50 μM ABDA, to achieve a final photocatalyst concentration of 50 mg dm−3. The suspensions were irradiated within a quartz cuvette with a 1 cm path length of up to 1 h using 465 nm LED array light source (FWHM ∼10 nm) with a light intensity of approximately 13 mW cm−2. Absorption spectra were recorded at given times during irradiation using a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrophotometer. The decrease in absorbance at 399 nm was used to quantify ABDA consumption, based on a calibration curve, and initial reaction rates were determined through linear regression. For experiments conducted in deaerated conditions, the suspensions were sealed and purged with argon gas for at least 15 min before irradiation began.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic hydroxyl radical generation via terephthalic acid hydroxylation
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of electron transfer was evaluated by measuring the photocatalytic generation of hydroxyl radical (HO•), detected through its reaction with non-fluorescent terephthalic acid to form fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH). CDs suspensions (50 mg dm−3) were prepared in a solution of 2 mM TA (in aqueous NaOH solution, pH ∼11). These suspensions were irradiated using a Thorlabs 365 nm LED (FWHM of 7.5 nm) with a light intensity of approximately 4 mW cm−2, contained within a quartz cuvette with a 1 cm optical path length. The formation of TAOH was monitored using a PerkinElmer LS-55 spectrofluorometer, with fluorescence emission recorded at 425 nm following excitation at 315 nm. Initial hydroxylation rates were determined from the linear region of fluorescence intensity increase, using a standard calibration curve for TAOH. Additional experiments were performed in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and EDTA to reach the final concentration of 0.1 mM H2O2, and 0.5 mM EDTA to investigate enhanced redox conditions. For deaerated experiments, the suspensions were sealed and purged with argon for at least 15 min before irradiation.

All CD samples appeared as dark brown flakes after freeze-drying. These flakes can be stored under ambient conditions without significant changes and readily dispersed in water without precipitation for up to 3 months at concentrations as high as 0.5 g dm−3. The purification process effectively removed interfering fluorophores (Fig. S2) and ensured that the observed photocatalytic activity was solely attributed to the CDs. This allowed for a valid and accurate comparison of their performance. In addition, the XRD analysis (Fig. S4) reveals irregular broad peaks characteristic of small amorphous clusters in all samples, with no detectable crystalline phases or impurities.

The elemental composition of all synthesized samples, as determined by CHNS analysis, is summarized in Table 2 . The pristine CA CDs primarily consist of carbon and the other expected major element is oxygen, though it was not directly detected in the analysis. The O-to-C ratio of approximately 1 closely aligns with the decomposition of processes of citric acid (O-to-C ratio of 1.17) through dehydration and decarboxylation [48,49]. No significant amounts of nitrogen and sulfur (less than 0.5 %) were found in the CA sample and thus can be used as a reliable reference for comparison with the doped materials. In the fractionated CA (CA-f), which has a reduced molecular mass of approximately 10–15 kDa, the hydrogen content remains similar to that of the pristine CA. However, there is a noticeable decrease in the carbon content and an increase in oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur percentages. This suggests that fractionation removes larger CDs, which typically have a smaller surface-to-core ratio, resulting in significantly lower measured carbon content than others. Alternatively, the fragmentation process may preferentially remove carbon atoms, potentially exposing more oxygen- and heteroatom-rich sites.

For the heteroatom-doped CDs, the N-doped sample using urea as a co-precursor (N–U sample) exhibited a high nitrogen content of approximately 15 %, indicating incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon framework (N-to-C ratio ca. 0.5). In the (N,S)-co-doped samples, varying amounts of nitrogen and sulfur were observed depending on a co-precursor and mass ratio. Thus, the doping process effectively introduced these heteroatoms in different proportions. These variations provide a solid basis for further investigating the role of heteroatom doping on the photocatalytic and optical properties of the CDs [50].

The FT-IR results, displayed in Fig. S5, confirm the presence of oxygen across all samples, as indicated by the CO stretching band at 1660 cm−1 and the broad O–H stretching peak between 3500 and 3100 cm−1 [51]. While this region may also correspond to N–H stretching, only the N–U sample exhibits an additional distinct peak at 3208 cm−1, corresponding to its higher nitrogen content [52], as confirmed by CHNS analysis (Table 2). Sulfur-containing CDs display characteristic peaks at 665 cm−1 and 1023 cm−1, attributed to C–S/C–S–C bending [53] and C–S stretching [54], respectively. The absence of a peak at 2580 cm−1 indicates the lack of –SH groups.

Fig. 3 presents both scanning and transmission electron microscopy images, along with the size distribution of selected CD samples. All samples exhibit a high degree of agglomeration and amorphous morphology (see also Fig. S6). DLS measurements confirmed that the secondary particle sizes of the samples are in the range of a few hundred nanometers (data not shown), while the primary particle sizes, measured from extremely diluted aqueous suspensions, are smaller than 6 nm. This result is consistent with the typical size range of CDs. Although most of the samples are amorphous, a crystalline lattice structure is visible in the N,S-Meth sample (Fig. 3, middle right), indicating partial crystallinity within this specific sample.

Fig. 4 presents STEM and EDS elemental mapping N–U (nitrogen-doped) and N,S-Meth (nitrogen and sulfur-doped). The N–U sample (upper panel) displays a highly agglomerated, amorphous structure, as evidenced by its diffuse and irregular morphology. The EDS elemental maps reveal a uniform distribution of carbon and nitrogen, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen into the CD structure. The presence of sodium and chlorine, though less concentrated and sparsely distributed, likely results from residual impurities following synthesis and purification. In contrast, the N,S-Meth sample (lower panel) exhibits a more crystalline or organized morphology, with clearer boundaries and a more defined structure compared to the N–U sample. The elemental mapping shows a uniform distribution of carbon that is consistent with the carbonaceous nature of the CDs. Nitrogen and sulfur are also well distributed, which indicates successful co-doping of these elements. The presence of oxygen suggests potential surface oxidation or functional groups.

The absorption spectra of all synthesized CDs are presented in Fig. S7. All samples exhibit absorption in the UV-A to blue light region, in which the N–U sample shows the highest absorption, followed by the N,S-Glu and N,S-Cys samples, and then the pristine CA. The remaining three samples display similar absorption characteristics but are noticeably less efficient than the first group. Notably, the fractionated CA-f sample exhibits even weaker absorption in the visible region compared to the original CA. This decline is likely due to the lower carbon content and an increase in band gap energy, causing a blue shift that results from the smaller quantum confinement in the fragmented structure.

Fig. 5 presents the steady-state fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs when excited by different wavelengths. All samples exhibit Stokes shift emissions, confirming their photoluminescent properties. Among them, the N–U sample once again stands out with the strongest fluorescence emission. All (N,S)-co-doped samples show comparable fluorescence efficiencies, which are still noticeably higher than that of pristine CA. These results strongly suggest that the incorporation of heteroatoms in CDs enhances their fluorescence properties. Notably, despite its lower absorption coefficient, the CA-f sample exhibits significantly enhanced fluorescence emission compared to the original CA. The effect of solvent concentration and acidic pH was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S8.

To investigate the photocatalytic activity of CDs for singlet oxygen (1O2) generation, commercially available ABDA was used as a selective water-soluble 1O2 probe that does not react with other ROS species. The basis of 1O2 detection is their reaction with ABDA to form an anthracene endoperoxide via a [4 + 2]-cycloaddition [55,56], as shown in the top left panel of Fig. 6 , and the resulting loss of the extended π-electron system and its characteristic spectroscopic properties. Although the formation of the anthracene endoperoxide is partly reversible upon heating, it can be regarded as irreversible at room temperature [55]. The absorption spectra of ABDA upon irradiation by LED@465 nm without photocatalyst show negligible changes, which ensure the exclusion of the photobleaching effect (Fig. S9). The change in characteristic absorption wavelength at 399 nm of ABDA can be monitored to assess the formation of endoperoxide, referred to as the generation of singlet oxygen.

Fig. 6a illustrates the ABDA transformation rate for all CD samples compared to a control reaction without a photocatalyst (sparse bar). It is evident that the transformation rate is enhanced in the presence of CD photocatalysts across all samples. Notably, all heteroatom-doped CDs show higher conversion rates than the undoped CA sample, with the exception of the N,S-Meth sample, which exhibits a fragile activity in this reaction. The other (N,S)-containing materials generally exhibit improved singlet oxygen production, approximately 1.5–2 times greater than CA. Interestingly, the N–U sample, which contains only nitrogen heteroatoms without sulfur, displays an exceptionally high conversion rate, nearly 5 times higher than CA (Fig. S10) and more than twice that of any (N,S)-containing sample. This makes it the most active material in the series for energy transfer-based singlet oxygen production. The singlet oxygen mechanism was confirmed by the dramatic decrease in transformation rate to near inactivity when the reaction was performed under deaerated conditions (Figs. S10–11). These findings align well with the fluorescent properties discussed earlier.

It is also noteworthy that while N,S-CysAm absorbs less light in the blue region than CA, its transformation rate is slightly higher. This contrasts with the CA-f sample, where the singlet oxygen production rate is lower than CA, despite a stronger emission signal. This evidence underlines that the steady-state optical properties of CDs such as the fluorescent emission might not directly correlate with their particular photoactivity. Additionally, while co-doping with nitrogen and sulfur may enhance photoactivity through a claimed synergistic effect compared to undoped CDs [17,19,20], the presence of sulfur does not necessarily guarantee superior performance. In fact, the highest activity was observed in the mono nitrogen-doped sample, rather than in the dual N/S-doped samples. However, within the dual N/S-doped samples, the efficiency of singlet oxygen production seems to correlate with the total nitrogen content, following the trend: (N,S-) Glu > Cys > CysAm > Meth.

Photocatalytic activity based on electron transfer was assessed by monitoring hydroxyl radical generation through the hydroxylation of TA under basic conditions [57,58]. Initially, all CD samples exhibited no significant difference from the control reaction without CDs, as shown in Fig. S12. This indicates that the CDs are inactive in photocatalytic electron transfer processes involving water and dissolved oxygen. If CDs act as a semiconductor, the inactivity is attributed to the CDs’ insufficient redox capability to either oxidize water or hydroxide ions via photogenerated holes in the conduction band (h + VB), which would generate hydroxyl radicals, or reduce dissolved oxygen via photogenerated electrons in the valence band (e – CB). This requires a minimum redox potential as outlined in Equations (1)–(3) (potential vs. NHE) [58–60]:
(1) H2O(l) + h + VB → HO• + H+ E 0 = +2.73 V
(2) HO− + h + VB → HO• E 0 = +1.90 V
(3) O2(aq) + e – CB → O2 •– E 0 = −0.18 V

To further investigate the photoactivity and identify any rate-limiting steps, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and EDTA were added to the starting solution as facilitated electron acceptor and donor, respectively. The results of the H2O2-assisted hydroxylation of TA are shown in Figs. S13 and 7b. Surprisingly, the previously least active N,S-Meth sample demonstrated an exceptionally high rate of hydroxyl radical generation compared to the other samples and the control (sparse bars). This suggests that N,S-Meth undergoes the electron transfer mechanism. The reason for this switching behavior is that hydrogen peroxide is more easily reduced than dissolved oxygen, as its reduction potential is more positive:
(4) H2O2 + e – CB → HO• + HO− E 0 = +0.80 V
In this scenario, electron transfer from the conduction band of CDs is facilitated, resulting in HO• production via the reductive pathway [57,58], thus rendering N,S-Meth photoactive by overcoming the reduction bottleneck that observed in the absence of H2O2. This also lowers the minimum band gap requirement to around 1.10 eV, based on the oxidation of hydroxide ions coupled with the reduction of hydrogen peroxide. Nevertheless, it should be noted that the hydroxyl radical production rate is far less effective than typical semiconductor photocatalysts, such as metal oxides, even when normalized for catalyst concentration [57,58,61].

The other materials that were active in energy transfer processes did not show enhanced hydroxyl radical production compared to the control reaction, and some samples with high singlet oxygen production efficiency—such as N–U, N,S-Glu, and N,S-Cys—actually showed a decrease in HO• generation rate. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that hydrogen peroxide can undergo homolytic cleavage upon UV irradiation, resulting in two hydroxyl radicals [62,63]. In our system, the low-intensity 365 nm LED (light density ∼4 mW cm−2) was employed in order to maintain a small number of background light-induced homolytic cleavage. Consequently, the control reactions without CDs exhibited a substantial increase in HO• generation rate, reaching approximately 65 nM min−1. However, in the presence of highly fluorescent CDs, the UV light was significantly quenched, reducing the photon flux reaching the peroxide molecules. This, in turn, led to decreased HO• production from peroxide cleavage. Consequently, it should be noted that the possibility of singlet oxygen generation via the electron transfer mechanism, as proposed by Nosaka et al. [64], can be definitively ruled out based on the ABDA experiment, as the electron transfer in these CDs is not effective.

EDTA is known as an excellent hole scavenger, particularly for facilitating oxidation processes in metal oxide semiconductors [57,65,66], due to its four carboxylic ligands that can efficiently bind to the metal oxide surface and promote electron transfer. However, in our system, the addition of EDTA did not improve performance in any of the experiments. In fact, it significantly reduced activity by more than half. This suggests that EDTA is ineffective in the tested CD systems, likely because it lacks strong surface attachment. Instead, smaller hydroxide ions are more effectively to access the surface of CDs. Consequently, EDTA competitively reacts with hydroxyl radicals [67], leading to a lower detected amount of TAOH.

To gain deeper insight into the differing photoactivities, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime (τ) of all samples. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in linear scale in Fig. 7 a, with the corresponding logarithmic scale in the inset, and the calculated lifetimes based on a bi-exponential fit [68,69] are plotted in Fig. 7b. Two parameters were fitted, corresponding to short-lived (<2.5 ns) and long-lived (>4 ns) photons, with their normalized distribution represented by scatter size and a color map. While most CDs had short-lived photons with lifetimes around 1.5 ns, the N–U sample exhibited a slightly longer short-lived lifetime of approximately 2.2 ns. Notably, the long-lived photons in all doped CD materials displayed significantly longer lifetimes compared to undoped CA. This result aligns well with the steady-state fluorescence emission observed earlier. N–U had the longest long-lived photon lifetime of more than 8 ns, with about 30 % of the total photon population, which corresponds perfectly with its outstanding singlet oxygen generation via the energy transfer mechanism. Furthermore, the efficiency of singlet oxygen production in the three (N,S)-containing samples excluding N,S-Meth also correlated directly with their long-lived lifetimes; the longer τ resulted in more efficient singlet oxygen production. However, CA-f did not follow this trend, possibly due to an increase in the short-lived photon fraction. This evidence suggests that the fractionation of pristine CDs is less effective in enhancing performance compared to the incorporation of heteroatoms. However, further investigation across a broader range of molecular masses is needed to validate this interpretation. Fractionation might still prove beneficial, particularly if the focus is shifted toward higher molecular mass fractions, for instance, by excluding lower molecular mass components.

While the fluorescence lifetime experiments support experimental results obtained from the energy transfer mechanism, the lack of singlet oxygen production in the N,S-Meth sample and its ability to undergo electron transfer, remains unclear. To further investigate, XPS analysis was performed on selected active materials to gain insights into surface species. The survey and high-resolution XPS data are presented in Figs. S13–15. Surface concentrations of chemical bonds, obtained from fitting the XPS data for all analyzed samples, are listed in Table 3 .

The surface species at a depth of 5 nm consisted of typical components commonly found in amorphous carbon dots. The surface-to-bulk distribution was calculated using carbon content as an internal reference. N/C and S/C ratios from XPS were subtracted from the total corresponding ratios obtained through CHNS analysis (Table 2). The percentage of surface N and S species is illustrated in Fig. 8 a. It was found that more than 65 % of the total nitrogen in all examined samples was located at the surface, with N,S-Glu showing up to 75 %. In contrast, the surface sulfur distribution ranged from 20 % to 35 % in sulfur-containing samples. Although these numbers do not directly correlate with photoactivity at first glance, it can be seen that when the surface N-to-S ratio is low (around 2), the lifetime distribution shifts to a greater proportion of long-lived photons, as observed in N,S-Cys and N,S-Meth, where the distribution between short- and long-lived photons is nearly equal. In contrast, in samples with a high surface N-to-S ratio, such as N,S-Glu (close to 4), the majority of photons (∼76 %) are short-lived. This suggests a relationship between the surface distribution of heteroatoms and the fluorescence lifetime characteristics.

The high-resolution XPS analysis and deconvolution fits of the S 2p spectra across the three (N,S)-containing samples are shown in Fig. 3b–d. The S 2p spectra were fitted with two doublet structures (doublet separation between 2 p3/2 and 2p1/2 is 1.18 eV). The first 2 p3/2 peak, centered at 163.4 eV, corresponds to C-SH organic bonds, while the second 2 p3/2 peak, centered at 165.9 eV, corresponds to C-(SO)–C organic groups [70,71]. Interestingly, the SO group is the dominant species only in the N,S-Meth sample (Fig. 8d), which is the sole material capable of generating ROS via electron transfer. The prominent presence of surface SO species may explain the switching from energy transfer to electron transfer mechanisms in CDs. This reasoning is further supported by the higher concentration of –OH and –COOH species in N,S-Meth compared to other (N,S)-containing samples (see Table 3), as these species also involve oxygen atoms bonded to non-carbon elements. Therefore, the preference for electron transfer in N,S-Meth is likely due to the rearrangement of bonded oxygen atoms with other heteroatoms, such as sulfur, on the surface, combined with the partial crystallinity (higher degree of ordering) observed in this sample, which is absent in other CDs. However, future work focusing on selective surface modifications, such as the adsorption of SO and related species on undoped and N-doped CDs, is necessary to conclusively validate this hypothesis.

In summary, a series of CDs with varying amounts of nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms were successfully synthesized via a facile thermolysis method using multiple precursors. The CDs underwent extensive purification to remove fluorophores and impurities, ensuring precise comparisons. The incorporation of heteroatoms generally enhanced the optical properties and energy transfer efficiency, as demonstrated by improved photocatalytic singlet oxygen production compared to undoped CDs. Among the samples, nitrogen-doped CDs (N–U) exhibited the highest activity and strongest fluorescence, attributed to their long-lived photon lifetimes, surpassing those of the N,S-co-doped samples. In contrast, the N,S-Meth sample, which exhibited partial crystallinity, showed minimal singlet oxygen production but demonstrated significant photocatalytic activity via electron transfer observed in H2O2-assisted hydroxyl radical generation.

The distinction between energy and electron transfer is proposed to originate from the presence of surface SO groups and other oxygen-containing species, which rearrange in a more ordered, crystalline phase. This phase behaves similarly to semiconductor photocatalysts and plausibly facilitates electron transfer rather than the traditional energy transfer observed in amorphous CDs. These findings suggest that the distribution of surface heteroatoms and specific bonding patterns play a critical role in determining whether CDs follow energy or electron transfer pathways, as well as their overall efficiency. This insight is crucial for advancing the understanding of CD photochemistry and lays the foundation for developing more efficient and tailored CD-based photocatalysts.
